structured_case.title: Patel v. Riverbend Grocers — premises liability slip-and-fall
relief_sought: Compensatory damages of $85,000 for medical expenses and lost wages
key_facts: On July 12, 2025 plaintiff slipped on a leaking refrigerator puddle in aisle 4 of defendant's store.
Store incident log shows a prior leak report from the same unit two days earlier with no repair ticket.
Surveillance video captured the puddle existing for at least 47 minutes before the fall.

cases.name: Williams v. Central Railroad
cases.holding_summary: Contributory negligence bars recovery where the plaintiff's conduct is a proximate cause of the injury and fails to exercise ordinary care.

cases.name: Holcomb v. Union Market
cases.holding_summary: A merchant owes invitees a duty to keep aisles reasonably safe; constructive notice arises where a hazard existed long enough that ordinary inspection would have discovered it.

cases.name: Bryant v. Ozark Theaters
cases.holding_summary: Business invitees are entitled to ordinary care; failure to remedy a known recurring hazard supports a negligence finding.

cases.name: Miller v. Pioneer Investments
cases.holding_summary: Fraud in the inducement requires a material misrepresentation, scienter, reasonable reliance, and resulting damages.

cases.name: Davis v. Ozark Manufacturing
cases.holding_summary: Termination without cause during a definite employment term breaches the contract; damages are measured by wages for the unexpired term minus mitigation earnings.

favorable_precedents.name: Holcomb v. Union Market
favorable_precedents.holding_summary: A merchant owes invitees a duty to keep aisles reasonably safe; constructive notice arises where a hazard existed long enough that ordinary inspection would have discovered it.

favorable_precedents.name: Bryant v. Ozark Theaters
favorable_precedents.holding_summary: Business invitees are entitled to ordinary care; failure to remedy a known recurring hazard supports a negligence finding.

unfavorable_precedents.name: Williams v. Central Railroad
unfavorable_precedents.holding_summary: Contributory negligence bars recovery where the plaintiff's conduct is a proximate cause of the injury and fails to exercise ordinary care.

predicted_outcomes.methodology: Bayesian-style estimate from CAP precedent sample plaintiff outcomes, adjusted for party role and precedent depth.

scenarios.description: Liability finding for plaintiff with a comparative-fault reduction of 10-25%.
Full compensatory award of $85,000 plus pain-and-suffering damages without fault reduction.
Defense verdict on lack of actual or constructive notice, or comparative fault ≥ 50%.

predicted_outcomes.likely_judgment_summary: Liability finding for plaintiff with a comparative-fault reduction of 10-25%.

win_strategies.title: Lock in constructive notice with video and incident log
actions: Authenticate the 47-minute video timeline through the store's loss-prevention custodian.
Subpoena maintenance tickets and refrigeration vendor records tied to the unit in aisle 4.
win_strategies.rationale: Constructive notice is the central liability question; the strongest evidence is contemporaneous and documentary.

win_strategies.title: Neutralize comparative-fault defense
actions: Develop expert testimony that the puddle was not open and obvious under typical store lighting.
Preserve plaintiff's footwear and emphasize ordinary shopping conduct at the time of fall.
win_strategies.rationale: Arkansas modified comparative fault can extinguish recovery if plaintiff's share reaches 50%.

win_strategies.title: Quantify damages with medical and wage proof
actions: Compile certified medical bills and treating-physician narrative through MMI.
Document missed shifts with payroll records and supervisor declarations.
win_strategies.rationale: Damages credibility frames settlement leverage and jury anchoring.

win_strategies.title: Preempt spoliation defense
actions: Send litigation hold letter immediately for video, logs, and maintenance records.
Move to compel preservation if defendant signals overwriting practices.
win_strategies.rationale: Loss of the 47-minute clip would gut the constructive-notice theory.

win_strategies.title: Frame settlement corridor
actions: Anchor demand to medical specials plus a multiplier reflecting Holcomb-line liability strength.
Define walk-away point before depositions to control cost exposure.
win_strategies.rationale: Slip-and-fall matters frequently resolve once liability proofs are crystallized.

favorable_judgment_tactics.detail: Move for partial summary judgment on duty and breach once video and logs are authenticated.
Frame the case as a narrow, record-based dispute about a single refrigeration unit and a documented prior report.
Tier damages between economic specials and non-economic harm to support a favorable partial judgment.
Use scenario probabilities to drive a pre-mediation demand that reflects comparative-fault risk.

complaint_outline.content: Anika Patel v. Riverbend Grocers Inc.
Plaintiff: Anika Patel; Defendant: Riverbend Grocers Inc.
Numbered paragraphs tying the prior leak report, the 47-minute video, and the fall to each element of duty, breach, causation, and damages.
Compensatory damages of $85,000; pain and suffering damages; costs of suit

proposed_exhibits.description: Surveillance video stills and chain-of-custody affidavit
Key precedent: 84 Ark. 17 (1928) Holcomb v. Union Market

pre_filing_checklist: Confirm three-year statute of limitations for negligence in Arkansas.
Verify filing fee and e-filing account for Pulaski County Circuit Court.
Attach civil cover sheet if required by local rules.
Review formatting requirements (margins, line numbering, word limits).

steps: Open e-filing portal for Circuit Court.
Upload PDF with embedded exhibits; pay filing fee.
Serve defendant per Rule 4 / local equivalent within required period.
how_to_file.note: This system does not e-file on your behalf.

top_precedents.name: Williams v. Central Railroad
top_precedents.headnotes: Contributory negligence bars recovery where the plaintiff's conduct is a proximate cause of the injury and fails to exercise ordinary care.
top_precedents.snippet: The railroad owed a duty of reasonable care at crossings, but the plaintiff's failure to stop, look, and listen when approaching a known dangerous crossing defeats the claim.

top_precedents.name: Holcomb v. Union Market
top_precedents.headnotes: A merchant owes invitees a duty to keep aisles reasonably safe; constructive notice arises where a hazard existed long enough that ordinary inspection would have discovered it.
top_precedents.snippet: Liquid had pooled on the market floor for nearly an hour with no inspection sweep; reasonable diligence would have discovered and removed the hazard.
